coredata.dc:title: Exceptional photo-elimination of antibiotic by a novel Z-scheme heterojunction catalyst composed of nanoscale zero valent iron embedded with carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-black TiO2
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xi
Wang, Xiangyu
Lynch, Iseult
Guo, Zhiling
Zhang, Peng
Wu, Lisi
Ning, Ping
Ren, Nanqi
coredata.dc:description: Passivation of nanoscale zero valent iron (nZVI, Fe0) impaired its longevity while black TiO2 (b-TiO2) suffered from restricted optical properties. Using a facile approach, a novel Z-scheme heterojunction catalyst (Fe0@CQDs-TiO2(b)) of nZVI decorated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) implanted into b-TiO2 was designed. Characterization results revealed the optical potential of the passivation coating of nZVI. The incorporation of CQDs stimulated the creation of active •OH during the dark reaction, and led to an accelerated mobility of photo-excited carriers of b-TiO2 and optimized its band gap (narrowing from 2.36 eV to 2.15 eV) during the light reaction. The photo-elimination capacity of metronidazole (MNZ) on Fe0@CQDs-TiO2(b) (99.36%) was 2.64, 8.25 and 1.34 fold beyond that on nZVI, b-TiO2 and Fe0@b-TiO2, respectively. The assembled material offered excellent adaptability to environmental substrates, in addition to being virtually unaffected by tap (95.62%) and river water (92.62%). The mechanism of MNZ degradation was elaborated, and the combination of density functional theory (DFT) calculations and LC–MS discerned 12 intermediates and 3 routes. Toxicity assessment of these products was conducted to ensure no inadvertent negative environmental impacts arose. This work proposed an original direction and mechanism for the application of passivation layers in nZVI-based materials for environmental restoration.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the workflow for facile assembly of Fe0 @CQDs-TiO2(b).
SEM images of (a) Fe0 and (b) Fe@C-Ti-2. (c) TEM, (d–e) HRTEM pictures and (f) mapping results for Fe@C-Ti-2.

(a) XRD results and (b) FT-IR spectra of as-synthesized catalysts. XPS patterns for (c) C 1 s and (d) O 1 s of composite before and after embedding with CQDs. (e) Fe 2p XPS patterns of Fe@C-Ti-2 before and after the lightless reaction.

(a) Photocatalytic experiments of as-prepared catalysts for MNZ. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of MNZ photodegradation catalyzed by Fe@C-Ti-2. (c) Pseudo-first-order kinetics, (d) associated reaction rate constants, and (e) revised pseudo-first-order kinetics for elimination of MNZ by as-synthesized catalysts. (f) Cycle tests of Fe@C-Ti-2 to confirm the recyclability of the catalytic function over many cycles of photo-elimination. (Test parameters: pH = 6.7, MNZ = 30 mg·L−1, catalyst dosage = 12 mg).

Effects of (a) MNZ concentration and (b) pH on MNZ elimination with Fe@C-Ti-2. Resistance of Fe@C-Ti-2 to environmental factors ((c) anions, humic acid and (d) various types of water bodies) in the process of eliminating MNZ. (Anions = 1 mmol·L−1).

(a) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent responses of the tested catalysts. (b) UV–vis DRS and their relevant (c) Tauc plots, as well as (d) VB-XPS of the nanomaterials. (e) Schematic representation of the position variation of the energy bands in the catalysts and the configuration mechanism of the heterojunction.
(a) Free radical quenching experiments of Fe@C-Ti-2 under visible-light irradiation. ESR spectra recognizing (b) •O2 −, (c) •OH and (d) h+ on Fe@C-Ti-2 respectively.
Schematic illustration of the potential mechanism of MNZ photodegradation by Fe@C-Ti-2.

(a) The molecular structure of MNZ with labeled atoms. (b) Distribution states of HOMO and LUMO, and (c) electrostatic potential (ESP)-mapped molecular surface of MNZ. (d) The associated charge distribution and Fukui index (f−,f+ and f0).
Relevant routes of MNZ degradation by Fe@C-Ti-2 based on the metabolites detected and the Fukui index.

(a) Bioconcentration factor, (b) mutagenicity, (c) acute toxicity LC50 (Daphnia magna, 48 h) and (d) growth inhibition IGC50 (T. Pyriformis, 48 h) of intermediates during the elimination of MNZ using Fe@C-Ti-2.
Comparison of the photodegradation efficiency of MNZ with catalysts from literature.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Passivation of nanoscale zero valent iron (nZVI, Fe0) impaired its longevity while black TiO2 (b-TiO2) suffered from restricted optical properties. Using a facile approach, a novel Z-scheme heterojunction catalyst (Fe0@CQDs-TiO2(b)) of nZVI decorated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) implanted into b-TiO2 was designed. Characterization results revealed the optical potential of the passivation coating of nZVI. The incorporation of CQDs stimulated the creation of active •OH during the dark reaction, and led to an accelerated mobility of photo-excited carriers of b-TiO2 and optimized its band gap (narrowing from 2.36 eV to 2.15 eV) during the light reaction. The photo-elimination capacity of metronidazole (MNZ) on Fe0@CQDs-TiO2(b) (99.36%) was 2.64, 8.25 and 1.34 fold beyond that on nZVI, b-TiO2 and Fe0@b-TiO2, respectively. The assembled material offered excellent adaptability to environmental substrates, in addition to being virtually unaffected by tap (95.62%) and river water (92.62%). The mechanism of MNZ degradation was elaborated, and the combination of density functional theory (DFT) calculations and LC–MS discerned 12 intermediates and 3 routes. Toxicity assessment of these products was conducted to ensure no inadvertent negative environmental impacts arose. This work proposed an original direction and mechanism for the application of passivation layers in nZVI-based materials for environmental restoration.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The code is available at https://github.com/patrickfan/GenAI4UQ.

ce:section.ce:para: Metronidazole (MNZ), a derivative of nitroimidazole, is used worldwide as a medicine to combat infections of anaerobic bacteria and protozoa, and as a feed additive to eliminate parasites [1,2]. Its popularity stems from its good efficacy, negligible side effects and acceptable price [1]. However, MNZ cannot be adequately utilized by organisms and as a result accumulates progressively in aquatic environments owing to its non-biodegradability and high solubility [3]. MNZ is mutagenic and carcinogenic and has been classified on the list of carcinogens by the World Health Organization (WHO) [4,5]. Hence, it is imperative to thoroughly eliminate MNZ from the aquatic environment.

Recently, photocatalytic technology has exhibited excellent application prospects for the efficient degradation of antibiotics due to its cost-effectiveness, outstanding efficiency and environmental compatibility [6]. For decades, TiO2 has been regarded as the most popular photocatalyst due to its excellent stability and non-toxicity. However, the widespread use of TiO2 as a catalyst is constrained by its low visible-light photo-activity [7]. Compared with TiO2, black TiO2 (b-TiO2) offers a smaller band gap and greater utilization of visible-light, making it a promising alternative. Rajaraman et al. mentioned that the defect species in b-TiO2 such as oxygen vacancies (OVs) and Ti3+ were responsible for its narrow band gap and broadened visible-light response range, which resulted in strengthened visible-light photocatalytic properties [7–9]. Nevertheless, b-TiO2 suffers from poor segregation of photo-excited carriers, which leads to restricted conductivity.

As a new family member of carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) display many useful characteristics, such as wide availability, simple preparation, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, easy functionalization, and excellent electronic storage and transfer capacity [10–13]. Owing to the excellent electron storage and transport capacity of CQDs, the conductivity of b-TiO2 and the segregation of its photo-induced electron-hole pairs can be boosted. Thus, the photocatalytic properties of b-TiO2 are expected to be further improved via combination with CQDs.

As a promising nanomaterial for eliminating antibiotics, nanoscale zero valent iron (nZVI) has been broadly researched stemming from its potent reduction capacity, magnetism and biocompatibility [14,15]. Support loading, in which nZVI is interspersed with carbonaceous surfaces such as biochar and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) with stable structures, is a capable method to overcome the aggregation of Fe0 particles caused by their inherent magnetism [16,17]. Besides, the surface of Fe0 gradually creates an oxide coating during the degradation of pollutants, which reduces the reactivity and longevity of nZVI [18–20]. Several works have revealed the main ingredient of this passivation layer is iron oxide (Fe2O3) [21–23]. Despite these challenges, Fe2O3 is regarded as an exceptional photocatalyst [24–26]. Based on this, we hypothesize that the passivation coating may be activated as a semiconductor and thus be anticipated to assemble a heterojunction with other semiconductors. Given this, we proposed to construct a high-performance visible-light photocatalyst via the combination of Fe0 and CQDs-TiO2(b). The coupling effect that confers high performance lies in (1) the doping of CQDs-TiO2(b) induces the high dispersion of Fe0 and thus exposes more available reactive centers; (2) the loading of Fe0 further optimizes the reduction capacity and conductivity of the composite; (3) the passivation layer on the surface of nZVI and CQDs-TiO2(b) build a heterojunction, exhibiting superior photocatalytic potential. To the best of our knowledge, the coupling effect of Fe0 and CQDs-TiO2(b) for catalysis of the photo-elimination of MNZ has not yet been explored. Moreover, the feasibility, structure and capability of the heterojunction among the oxide layer and CQDs-TiO2(b), and the related actions of CQDs have not been previously reported.

An innovative photocatalyst of Fe0 @CQDs-TiO2(b) with high-performance Z-scheme heterojunctions was designed for the enhanced photo-elimination of MNZ. The morphology and structural characteristics of Fe0 @CQDs-TiO2(b) were characterized. The photocatalytic potential, optical properties of the composite and in-situ fabrication of the heterojunction were thoroughly investigated. The mechanism of photo-elimination was systematically clarified. Moreover, the associated elimination routes were proposed by combining density functional theory (DFT) calculations and LC-MS results of the degradation intermediates and products, and toxicity assessments targeting the degradation products were performed to ensure the overall environmental safety of the catalysts.
Details of materials employed in all experiments were provided in the Supplementary Material.

The synthesis of b-TiO2 was performed according to the method previously reported from our study team [27]. CQDs-TiO2(b) was prepared via a solvothermal process. In detail, pre-determined amounts of citric acid and urea were separately dissolved into 15 mL of ultrapure water (UPW). After a homogeneous solution was formed under magnetic stirring, the as-prepared b-TiO2 powders were added to the solution. Subsequently, these samples were sonicated and stirred for 45 min to assure the full dispersion of b-TiO2 in the CQDs precursor. Next, the samples were added to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and held at 180 °C for 5 h. Then, these samples were cooled overnight and washed 3 times with UPW. Finally, CQDs-TiO2(b) particles were obtained after these samples were dried overnight in an oven at 60 °C.

Fe0 @CQDs-TiO2(b) was synthesized via a facile liquid reduction process in an anaerobic box (gas atmosphere: 99.99% nitrogen), as shown schematically in Fig. 1. Briefly, FeSO4·7 H2O was completely dissolved in 40 mL of UPW, then 1 g of CQDs-TiO2(b) particles were poured into this solution and stirred for 20 min under continuous magnetic stirring to ensure that Fe2+ was fully and uniformly adsorbed onto the CQDs-TiO2(b). The excess KBH4 solution was slowly titrated into this solution and the mixture was stirred consecutively for 30 min. Then the mixture was filtered and washed 3 times successively using UPW, ethanol, and acetone. Ultimately, these as-synthesized samples were dried and kept in one anaerobic desiccator (gas atmosphere: 99.99% nitrogen). The mass ratio of FeSO4·7 H2O to CQDs-TiO2(b) was fixed at 1:1. In order to achieve optimized photocatalytic performance, the dosing ratio of CQDs and b-TiO2 in CQDs-TiO2(b) was explored. Among them, samples with mass ratios of CQDs of 0.25, 0.5, and 1 wt% were denoted as C-Ti-1, C-Ti-2, and C-Ti-3, respectively. The corresponding composites of Fe0 and CQDs-TiO2(b) were named Fe@C-Ti-1, Fe@C-Ti-2, and Fe@C-Ti-3 separately. For comparison, Fe0 and Fe0 @b-TiO2 were synthesized in the same way without the addition of CQDs-TiO2(b) and CQDs, respectively. Moreover, Fe@C-Ti after 30 min of dark reaction was denoted as Fe2O3 @C-Ti.

The photodegradability of MNZ by Fe0 @CQDs-TiO2(b) under visible-light irradiation was measured using a photochemical reactor (300 W xenon lamp, 420 nm cut-off filter, ZQ-GHX-V, China). First, 12 mg of Fe0 @CQDs-TiO2(b) particles were added to 60 mL of MNZ solution −1) and after 30 min of magnetic stirring under lightless (dark) conditions until an adsorption–desorption equilibrium was obtained. Afterwards, the photodegradation test was performed under visible-light illumination. Then a 1 mL aliquot was acquired, filtered and diluted (10 times) successively at the given time intervals. Finally, the content of MNZ was determined by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800PC, China) at a particular wavelength (319 nm). The degradation efficiency of MNZ (DE, %) was calculated according to formula (1):
(1) DE (%) = [(C
0 − C
t ) / C
0 ] × 100
where C
0 is the initial concentration of MNZ, and C
t represents the concentration of MNZ at time t (min)·Additionally, the photodegradation stability of Fe0@CQDs-TiO2(b) was investigated by carrying out a cycle test (5 cycles)·Moreover, a series of trapping agents (dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), ammonium oxalate monohydrate (AO), and methanol) were deployed to infer the critical species produced during the photo-elimination of MNZ·
The employed characterization techniques, related operations and parameters were available in the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and construction of as-synthesized materials
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the dominant photodegradation potential of Fe@C-Ti-2 (the material with the CQDs mass of 0.5 wt%), as reflected in Section 3.2, this material was selected for subsequent investigation. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were employed to scrutinize the morphology and construction of the nanomaterials. The natural magnetism of Fe0 particles leads to their mutual adhesion and thus they exhibit chain-like structures ( Fig. 2a), as discussed in previous literature [20]. Surprisingly, as illustrated in Fig. 2b, the spherical shapes and discernible boundaries of iron particles are observed even after they are embedded into CQDs-TiO2(b) supports. Combined with Figs. 2a, 2b and S1, it is clear that the fluffy structure and abundant inter-particle channels of C-Ti-2 particles (Fig. S1) offer convenience for domain confinement and dispersion of nZVI particles. The more distinct boundary of this composite compared with that of bare iron clearly facilitates the availability of more reactive sites, which may favor the adsorption and subsequent removal of MNZ molecules by the composite [6]. As observed from the TEM records of Fe@C-Ti-2 (Fig. 2c), the above features are reconfirmed, and the nZVI particles are excellently dispersed. In the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (Fig. 2d), dark carbon quantum dots are observed attached to black TiO2 nanocrystals, which is accredited to the solvothermal treatment. Furthermore, the HRTEM image (Fig. 2e) at 5 nm scale again discloses the presence of nZVI, CQDs and b-TiO2 nanocrystals in the composite due to their 0.20 (110), 0.32 (002) and 0.35 nm (101) lattice spacings, respectively. The operation of the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping technique (Fig. 2f) displays the even allocation of Fe, Ti, Mn, O, C and N in the complex, and also highlights the configuration of nitrogen-doped CQDs and the clear distribution of Fe0. Coupled with the observations in Fig. 2, it can be confirmed that an Fe0 @CQDs-TiO2(b) catalyst is successfully synthesized, and its obtained expanded boundary and the high dispersion of Fe0 contribute to the enhanced removal of contaminants presented subsequently.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to record the preparation procedure and compositional variation of the composite. As presented in Fig. 3a, the characteristic diffraction peaks of nZVI at 44.8° and 82.1° are matched with the (110) and (211) crystal surfaces in JCPDS No. 87–0721 [28]. The prepared b-TiO2 nanocrystal exhibits a typical anatase structure via comparison with the multiple characteristic peaks of JCPDS No. 21–1272 [29]. However, although CQDs are incorporated into the b-TiO2 nanocrystals, the characteristic peaks of JCPDS No. 41–1487 corresponding to CQDs are not evident in the XRD pattern of C-Ti-2 [30]. This result is credited to the overlap of the characteristic diffraction peak of CQDs (002) and b-TiO2 (101) near 2θ = 26° as well as to the trace percentage of CQDs in C-Ti-2 (0.5 wt%). After nZVI particles are deposited onto the CQDs-TiO2(b) supports, the XRD result of Fe@C-Ti-2 displays a broad peak with weak intensity corresponding to the (110) facet of Fe0 and unaltered characteristic peaks representing anatase. These confirm that the Fe0 particles in Fe@C-Ti-2 appear in a highly dispersed state as illustrated in the SEM and TEM images, and the constitution of the b-TiO2 nanocrystals is not disrupted by the doping with Fe0. Interestingly, a new species is generated after a half-hour dark reaction of Fe@C-Ti-2 in MNZ solution, followed by a nearly total losses of the characteristic peaks of nZVI. The diffraction peaks of this species, situated at 30.2°, 35.6°, 43.2° 57.3° and 62.9°, are allocated to the (206), (119), (0012), (1115) and (4012) crystal surfaces of Fe2O3 (JCPDS No. 25–1402), separately. Meanwhile, the anatase phase remains intact. It can be induced that the exterior of nZVI particles is encircled by a dense oxide coating (Fe2O3) after the lightless reaction.

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was utilized to reveal the functional groups and interactions in the catalysts. As depicted in Fig. 3b, there are some co-existing functional groups in Fe0 @b-TiO2 and Fe@C-Ti-2. Among them, the broad peak at 3420 cm−1 belongs to the stretching mode of O-H of the adsorbed water [31,32]. Besides, the absorption peaks at 1040, 1400 and 1630 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C-O, CC and CO, accordingly, and the peaks at 2850 and 2920 cm−1 illustrate the existence of C-H [30,31,33,34]. The appearance of these carbon-related functional groups may indicate the interference of extraneous carbon. In the low wavenumber region, the absorption bands near 470 cm−1 and at 630 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration modes of Ti-O-Ti and Fe-O, demonstrating the construction of b-TiO2 and the interaction between it and Fe0 [34–36]. Notably, a new intense absorption band emerges in the 1400–860 cm−1 region after the doping of CQDs into Fe0 @b-TiO2, where the bands located near 1200 and 1000 cm−1 refer to C-N and Fe-O-C bonds, separately [30–32,36]. These bonds validate the formation of N-doped CQDs and their association with nZVI and b-TiO2, in line with the findings of the elemental mapping, HRTEM and XRD.

The incorporation of nitrogen-doped CQDs and the generation of the passivation layer of nZVI were also evidenced with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S2) of Fe@C-Ti-2 reaffirms the successful assembly of this composite. As presented in Fig. 3c, the peaks at 288.27, 286.15 and 284.80 eV in Fe0 @b-TiO2 are distributed to CO, C-O and CC/C-C, while an extra peak (285.31 eV) for the C-N bond appears in Fe@C-Ti-2 [31,32]. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, the O 1 s XPS spectrum of Fe0 @b-TiO2 is deconvoluted into individual peaks representing the CO/C-OH (532.36 eV), O-H (531.28 eV) and Fe-O/Ti-O (529.75 eV) bonds, respectively. An additional peak (530.13 eV) belonging to the Fe-O-C bond is shown in the spectrum of Fe@C-Ti-2 [36–38]. Combining the C 1 s and O 1 s XPS spectra, a tight link between Fe0, CQDs and b-TiO2 is reconfirmed, as reflected in the FT-IR spectra. As for the Fe 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 3e), prior to the dark reaction, it is deconvoluted into 3 independent peaks, accordingly, the peaks located at 724.12 and 710.48 eV are allocated to iron oxide and the peak at 707.39 eV belongs to nZVI [21,39]. This agrees with the earlier reports that the fresh nZVI has a thin oxide film on its surface [20,22]. Nevertheless, after 30 min of lightless reaction, the characteristic peak of Fe0 vanishes, while that of Fe2O3 persists and the peak intensity increases, indicating the thickness of the oxide coating grows further.

Remarkably, both XRD and XPS results prove the nZVI surface is wrapped by a dense oxide layer after the dark reaction. As reported before, this passivation coating can be detrimental to the activity and lifetime of nZVI by blocking the connection between it and contaminants [18–20]. This implies that the oxide coating exhibits an inhibitory role on pollutant removal in the lack of driving forces. Hence, our research skillfully applies photocatalysis to stimulate the potential of the passivation coating as a semiconductor, which is a promising route to fabricate a heterojunction among the coating and the CQDs-TiO2(b) for the efficient elimination of MNZ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-elimination capacity and practical employment potential
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of nanomaterials towards MNZ was measured by a series of photocatalytic experiments ( Fig. 4a). The content of MNZ exhibits a negligible drop in the absence of catalysts, indicating its high stability under natural conditions. Nevertheless, the addition of catalysts induces the elimination of MNZ. Due to the presence of Fe0, there is a visible decrease in MNZ content during the dark reaction, and subsequently, the MNZ content is reduced slightly (37.66% of DE within 75 min). This is attributed to the generation of a passivation coating on the surface of Fe0 after the dark reaction, which prevents contact between Fe0 and MNZ and slows the reaction. At 45 min of visible-light radiation, b-TiO2 and C-Ti-2 exhibit 120.04% and 14.44% degradation efficiencies for MNZ, respectively. The results indicate that both of them have certain photocatalytic activities and demonstrate the favorable effect of CQDs doping on b-TiO2. For the composites, degradation efficiencies of 74.42%, 60.28%, 99.36% and 46.68% are obtained for Fe0 @b-TiO2, Fe@C-Ti-1, Fe@C-Ti-2 and Fe@C-Ti-3, separately, within 45 min of visible-light illumination. And the confined removal (84.76%) of MNZ on Fe@C-Ti-2 under lightless conditions of the whole process highlights the emphasis of illumination (Fig. S3). The degradation performance of the composites is notably higher than that of the single catalyst. For Fe@C-Ti-1, the insufficient addition of CQDs struggles to sufficiently intensify the Fenton-like process of nZVI and may instead mask the active sites of nZVI and b-TiO2. This affects the adsorption and reduction properties of the composite to MNZ molecules in the dark reaction phase, leading to a slight decline in the performance of Fe@C-Ti-1 relative to Fe0 @b-TiO2 [12]. Conversely, excess CQDs may severely block the active sites and channels of nZVI and b-TiO2, resulting in a drastic decline in the adsorption and reduction properties of Fe@C-Ti-3 [13]. Conspicuously, the photodegradation efficiency of MNZ on Fe@C-Ti-2 is 2.64 and 8.25 fold higher than that on bare Fe0 and b-TiO2, separately, indicating that the composite has excellent photocatalytic potential. Moreover, the DE on Fe@C-Ti-2 is 1.21 and 1.34 times as high as that on Fe0 @b-TiO2 in the dark reaction and the whole reaction respectively, fully demonstrating the critical role of appropriate amounts of CQDs for the enhanced elimination of MNZ. Furthermore, outstanding photocatalytic performance is obtained by Fe0 @CQDs-TiO2(b) in comparison with catalysts reported previously ( Table 1), implying that Fe0 @CQDs-TiO2(b) is a potential nanomaterial for the efficient removal of metronidazole.

UV–vis absorption spectra were applied to analyze the constructive decomposition and variation of MNZ on Fe@C-Ti-2. As the elimination reaction proceeds (Fig. 4b), the intensity of the characteristic absorption peak of MNZ positioned at 319 nm decreases noticeably. Meanwhile, the absorption band between 200 and 250 nm also has obvious variations. These phenomena reveal the remarkable photodegradation of MNZ and the generation of related intermediates driven by Fe@C-Ti-2.

A pseudo-first-order kinetics model was employed to depict the photodegradation of MNZ by the nanomaterials under visible-light illumination. As presented in Fig. 4c, all the nanomaterials exhibit a goodness of fit exceeding 0.9000, meaning that the model is suitable for describing the photodegradation procedure of MNZ. Besides, the absolute value of the slope of the fitted line for Fe@C-Ti-2 is the highest among all tested catalysts, further confirming its exceptional photocatalytic potential. Reaction rate constant k was utilized to quantify the photocatalytic performance of the nanomaterials for MNZ degradation (Fig. 4d). The k value of Fe@C-Ti-2 is the highest among all the tested nanomaterials, and its k value (0.0700 min−1) is 16.28 times higher than that of Fe0 @b-TiO2 (0.0043 min−1). These results reveal the essential role of CQDs in the strengthened photodegradation of MNZ, as evidenced in the photocatalytic experiments. Nevertheless, for Fe0-based nanomaterials, the role of Fe0 in the dark reaction step includes the adsorption and reduction of pollutants. Such a feature implies the pseudo-first-order kinetics model is inappropriate for this step, owing to its inability to account for both adsorption and reduction processes. To address this, a revised pseudo-first-order kinetics model (equation (S5) in Text S3) is proposed [47]. The goodness of fit of the fitted curves is above 0.9900 for all materials, demonstrating that the modified model can be applied to characterize the elimination of MNZ during the dark reaction step in Fe0-based catalysts. As illustrated in Fig. 4e, the k
a values of Fe@C-Ti-1, Fe@C-Ti-2, and Fe@C-Ti-3 sequentially reduce, and there is a similar trend in their specific surface areas (Table S1; 66.4217, 62.0073, and 46.6050 m2·g−1, respectively), which may be related to the increment of the loading of CQDs. While Fe@C-Ti-2 (0.1998 cm3·g−1) exhibits the highest pore volume among the three materials, which favors the transport of MNZ molecules. Relative to other Fe0-based nanomaterials, Fe@C-Ti-2 has the highest value of k
r , again proving the intermediate level of CQDs facilitates the reduction property of the composite. The stability and recoverability of Fe@C-Ti-2 were inspected by cycle tests of MNZ photodegradation. As shown in Fig. 4f, the DE of Fe@C-Ti-2 to MNZ is reduced by less than 10% after 5 cycles. This finding evidences its excellent stability. Furthermore, benefiting from the intrinsic magnetism of Fe@C-Ti-2 (Fig. S4, 24.70 emu·g−1), the composite can be extracted from treated water by an applied magnetic field (Fig. S5) and exhibits favorable recoverability after being cleaned and recycled with UPW and minor quantities of KBH4. These unique properties indicate the potential for extended application of Fe0 @CQDs-TiO2(b) in the treatment of MNZ-contaminated wastewater.

Impacts of MNZ content, pH, dosage, anions, humic acid (HA) and water quality on MNZ photo-elimination were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 5a, 20 mg·L−1 of MNZ is totally eliminated by Fe@C-Ti-2 after only half an hour of light exposure, and 67.09% of MNZ (50 mg·L−1) can be removed after 45 min of illumination. A series of pH gradient experiments (Fig. 5b) reveal that extreme alkaline environments are unfavorable to the performance release of Fe@C-Ti-2. And a catalyst dosage of 12 mg is deemed to be a sensible choice (Fig. S6). Surprisingly, the performance inhibition of this photocatalytic system by anions and HA is limited (Fig. 5c), and even the most influential H2PO4 − holds an efficiency of more than 75%. Tap water and river water containing abundant ions, organic matter and microorganisms may drastically affect the potential of the photocatalyst. However, the photo-elimination efficiency of this photocatalytic system (Fig. 5d) is barely impacted by tap water (95.62%) and river water (92.62%). These outcomes amply support the superior MNZ removal capacity and promising employment potential of this photocatalytic system.

As discussed above, CQDs make a vital contribution to the remarkably enhanced degradation ability exhibited by Fe@C-Ti-2 in both the dark and light reactions. Moreover, the phenomenon whereby the slope of the elimination curve of Fe@C-Ti-2 tends to 0 after half an hour of lightless reaction is analyzed. Due to the coexistence of adsorption and reduction in this reaction system, the occurrence of this phenomenon involves not only the adsorption–desorption equilibrium of MNZ, but should also be ascribed to the formation of a dense passivation layer on the nZVI surface of the reacted catalyst and thus the obstruction of contact between Fe0 and MNZ by this layer, as attested by XRD and XPS. However, the slope of the elimination curve steepens dramatically under visible-light illumination and the k value of Fe@C-Ti-2 is 43.75 times larger than that of C-Ti-2 in the photoreaction, implying that Fe2O3 with weak redox potential is another key component of the composite, and it may be excited by visible-light to interact intensely with the CQDs-TiO2(b) support, leading to the obviously improved elimination of MNZ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of as-prepared Z-scheme heterojunction composite
ce:section.ce:para: From the results of the degradation experiments, it is clear that photocatalysis broadens the potential of Fe@C-Ti-2 for the effective elimination of MNZ. Accordingly, the optical properties of catalysts were proven by diverse techniques. As displayed in Fig. 6a, the radius of the impedance curve of C-Ti-2 is obviously reduced relative to that of b-TiO2, and the curve radius of Fe2O3 @C-Ti-2 is declined further. It is evident from the inset that the photocurrent intensity of these materials is ranked as Fe2O3 @C-Ti-2 > C-Ti-2 > b-TiO2. These results support the hypothesis that the intervention of CQDs and oxide coatings can considerably diminish the impedance and enhance the transport of photo-excited charges in b-TiO2 nanocrystals. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra in Fig. S7 also illustrate the weakening of the PL intensity of b-TiO2 with the embedding of CQDs, whereas Fe2O3 @C-Ti-2 has a lower intensity relative to the previous two samples. This implies that Fe2O3 @C-Ti-2 obtains a strengthened separation efficiency of the photo-excited electron-hole pairs. From these characterization data, it is derived that the dramatically elevated segregation and transfer rate of photo-generated charges is achieved by Fe2O3 @C-Ti-2, which stems from the built-in electric field (IEF) created among the passivation coating and C-Ti-2.

The outstanding visible-light absorption properties of the passivation coating of nZVI and b-TiO2 nanocrystals are validated with ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) (Fig. 6b). Besides, the slope of the absorption band of C-Ti-2 between 600 and 800 nm is smaller compared with that of b-TiO2, which confirms that the insertion of CQDs further boosts the visible-light utilization of b-TiO2. Relying on their remarkable visible-light absorption, these materials possess narrow band gap energies (E
g ), as reflected by the Tauc plots in Fig. 6c. The band gap energies of Fe2O3, b-TiO2 and C-Ti-2 are 1.93, 2.36 and 2.15 eV, respectively; clearly, the narrower band gap of C-Ti-2 relative to that of b-TiO2 is a result of the doping with CQDs. The band gap of a semiconductor is determined by the difference between its valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB). As presented in Fig. 6d, the valence band energies (E
VB ) of Fe2O3, b-TiO2 and C-Ti-2 are quantified via the VB-XPS technique, and their values are 1.37, 2.27 and 2.49 eV, respectively. Consequently, the conduction band energies (E
CB ) of the three are − 0.56, − 0.09 and 0.34 eV separately, in view of the equation E
g = E
VB − E
CB .

It is notable that the positions of the valence and conduction bands of b-TiO2 vary before and after doping with CQDs, as also indicated in Fig. 6e. The valence band of b-TiO2 (2.27 eV) is located at E(•OH/H2O), while that of C-Ti-2 (2.49 eV) exhibits stronger oxidation ability relative to that of b-TiO2. In contrast, the reduction property of the conduction band in C-Ti-2 (0.34 eV) is weaker than that in b-TiO2 (−0.09 eV), yet the distance from its CB to the VB of Fe2O3 is further shortened, which is favorable for the fabrication of staggered heterojunctions. Upon contact of the oxide coating with C-Ti-2, the IEF and energy band bending are initiated [45,48,49]. The generation of •O2 − and •OH under visible-light illumination (as evidenced by ESR) signals the transfer of excited electrons on the CB of C-Ti-2 to the VB of Fe2O3 and the reconfiguration with the available holes on Fe2O3, which denotes the fabrication of a Z-scheme heterojunction. These evidence reveal the introduction of CQDs optimizes the composite in several ways, including in terms of light absorption, charge segregation and transportation, energy band modulation and heterostructure fabrication. Also, the superior optical performance of the Z-scheme heterojunction structured by the oxide coating of nZVI and C-Ti-2 is confirmed.

The employment of free radical quenching experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra could help to judge the conformation of heterojunctions thoroughly and elucidate the elimination mechanism comprehensively. As shown in Fig. 7a and its inset, 96.15% of the removal efficiency towards MNZ is attained with Fe@C-Ti-2 in the photo-reaction without scavenger addition. The addition of p-BQ and methanol, instead, causes mere 8.04% and 27.27% of elimination efficiency respectively, indicating that both •O2 − and •OH are crucial elements affecting MNZ degradation. Photo-excited hole (h+) becomes a subsidiary impact factor due to AO accounting for 66.04% of MNZ removal, but the effect of e− reflected by DMSO is not significant. Based on this, ESR spectra verifying the presence and function of •O2 −, •OH and h+ were extracted. As depicted in Fig. 7b, the peak intensities of 1:1:1:1 are noted with visible-light illumination for 15 min versus no signal in darkness. This distinction justifies the production of •O2 − during the photo-reaction. From Fig. 7c, the signal symbolizing •OH with peak intensities of 1:2:2:1 appears in both dark and light reactions, which reveals the generation of •OH in both stages. Notably, •OH peaks are not found with Fe0 @b-TiO2 compared with the obvious characteristic peaks in Fe@C-Ti-2 under darkness. This finding supports the fact that the addition of CQDs boosts the production of •OH in the dark reaction, which is direct evidence for the enhanced MNZ elimination capacity of Fe@C-Ti-2 relative to that of Fe0 @b-TiO2 during the dark reaction. Moreover, as displayed in Fig. 7d, characteristic TEMPO peaks with intensities of 1:1:1 are detected under lightless conditions, whereas these peaks are nearly missing under visible-light radiation, affirming the massive production of photo-generated holes in the photo-reaction. These arising reactive species are indeed responsible for the effective elimination of MNZ.

A composite catalyst of nanoscale zero valent iron encapsulated by CQDs-TiO2(b) nanocrystals exhibits promising elimination performance for MNZ, and the involved mechanism is depicted in Fig. 8. As stated above, reduction and photo-elimination serve as two procedures for MNZ degradation by Fe0 @CQDs-TiO2(b). Under a half-hour of lightless conditions, the removal of MNZ benefits from the inherent high reduction capacity of nZVI. After the catalyst is placed in the contaminated water, MNZ molecules are gradually adsorbed onto the catalyst and transferred to its active center. Due to the induction of a Fenton-like reaction, the contact of nZVI with dissolved oxygen (DO) and hydrogen ions in water leads to the formation of ferrous ions and hydrogen peroxide, followed by their further reaction to generate ferric ions and •OH [50,51]. As evidenced above, the doping of CQDs raises the production of •OH by facilitating the Fenton-like process. The details of the dark reaction are given in Eqs. (2)–(4) below.
(2) MNZ + Fe@C-Ti-2 → MNZ-Fe@C-Ti-2
(3) nZVI + O2 + 2 H+ → Fe (Ⅱ) + H2O2 → Fe (Ⅲ) + •OH + OH−
(4) •OH + MNZ-Fe@C-Ti-2 → intermediates → H2O + CO2 + NO3 −

During visible-light illumination, the photocatalytic reaction is credited as the dominant mechanism for MNZ elimination. Upon excitation of Fe2O3 and C-Ti-2 by visible-light, active electrons migrate from their VB to CB and leave holes at corresponding spots on their VB. Then, the free electrons on the CB of C-Ti-2 are moved and combined with the holes on the VB of Fe2O3. Afterwards, the highly separated and intensely reactive e− on the CB of Fe2O3 interacts with DO in the contaminated water to generate •O2 − because of the more negative potential of its CB (−0.56 eV) versus E (O2/•O2 −) = −0.33 eV [4,49]. Externally, some free electrons can reduce ferric ions to ferrous ions for the recycling of iron species. Concurrently, h+ with the strong oxidation property of the VB on C-Ti-2 contacts with OH−/H2O for forming •OH, which profits from its more positive potential (2.49 eV) compared with E (•OH/OH−) = 1.99 eV and E (•OH/H2O) = 2.27 eV [48,52]. Furthermore, some holes can react directly with MNZ and thus degrade it into small molecules. From the above findings, it is clear that an efficient Z-scheme heterojunction is assembled by Fe2O3 and C-Ti-2. Owing to the superiority of this heterojunction, eventually, the vibrant •O2 −, •OH and h+ generated can disintegrate MNZ into water, carbon dioxide and nitrate, etc. The proposed photo-elimination mechanism is listed in Eqs. (5)–(12).
(5)
hv + Fe2O3 → h+ + e−
(6)
hv + C-Ti-2 → h+ + e−
(7) e−-CB (C-Ti-2) → h+-VB (Fe2O3)
(8) e−-CB (Fe2O3) + O2 → •O2 −
(9) e− + Fe (Ⅲ) → Fe (Ⅱ)
(10) h+-VB (C-Ti-2) + OH−/H2O → •OH
(11) h+-VB (C-Ti-2) + MNZ → intermediates
(12) •O2 −/•OH/h+ + MNZ → intermediates → H2O + CO2 + NO3 −

As stated already, the incorporation of CQDs assists Fe0 @CQDs-TiO2(b) to gain exceptional degradation performance towards MNZ. The functions of CQDs are reflected in the following aspects: (1) the insertion of CQDs induces the generation of active •OH during the dark reaction via accelerating the Fenton-like procedure; (2) the CQDs reinforce the segregation and relocation efficiency of photo-induced charges in b-TiO2 nanocrystals; (3) the CQDs improve the light absorption capacity of b-TiO2, as well as shrink and optimize its band gap. Therefore, the utilization of CQDs holds potential significance for the thorough treatment of MNZ contaminated wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT calculations, pathways and toxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para: DFT calculation was an effective method for probing the degradation potential and sites of attack on MNZ, and LC-MS technique could identify MNZ and its degradation products. Thus, they were united to systematically clarify the reliable intermediates and potential routes of MNZ photodegradation using Fe@C-Ti-2. As illustrated in Fig. 9a–c, the labeled chemical structure, highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and electrostatic potential (ESP) of MNZ are captured. In the frontier orbital theory, HOMO and LUMO are the key positions of chemical reactions, and represent electron-losing and electrophilic abilities, accordingly. Also, they are situated around the ring of the MNZ molecule, signifying the proneness of MNZ to ring-opening reactions. As evidenced by the ESP-mapped molecular surface of MNZ, 3 C, 4 N, 7 C and 11 C are positive ESPs, while 2 C, 17 O and 21 O present negative ESPs. Such a charged feature allows for the contact of MNZ with the catalyst. However, it is hard for HOMO and LUMO to exactly quantify the reaction potential of each atom, so the Fukui index, precisely forecasting electrophilic attack (f−), nucleophilic attack (f+) and radical attack (f0), is imported and calculated in Fig. 9d. Among them, the charge distribution values and the colored parts correspond to the ESP exhibition as well as the HOMO and LUMO regions separately, which indicates the rationality and guidance of the quantitative data. The active species involved in this system with respect to MNZ degradation contain •O2 −, •OH and h+, in which, •O2 − and h+ are associated with the values of f+ and f− respectively, while •OH is linked to f− and f0 [53,54].

From the findings of liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS, Fig. S8), 12 intermediates are detected. By coupling these with the Fukui index, the chemical structures of the corresponding substances are discerned (Table S2), and 3 MNZ elimination pathways are finally proposed ( Fig. 10) [4,40,42,55–57].

In route Ⅰ, the N-ethanol group of MNZ is oxidized by •OH to yield m/z = 185 due to high f− and f0 values (15 H and 16 H). Then, the sensitivity of 9 H (f− = 0.1153, f0 = 0.0646), 11 C (f− = 0.0342, f0 = 0.0211) and 19 N (f− = 0.0166, f0 = 0.0123) contributes to the decarboxylation and hydroxylation processes creating m/z = 114 via the attack of •OH and h+. Besides, m/z = 185 can produce m/z = 155 through a hydroxylation reaction, which further forms m/z = 116 via the attack of •OH toward 5 H, 7 C, and 11 C. Subsequently, m/z = 116 is decomposed to m/z = 84 by •O2 −. For route Ⅱ, owing to the high Fukui indices of 19 N (f− = 0.0166,f+ = 0.0081, f0 = 0.0123) and 21 O (f− = 0.0252,f+ = 0.0233, f0 = 0.0242), they are susceptible to the attack of •O2 − and •OH, generating m/z = 125. Thereafter, on the one hand, the product sequentially generates m/z = 116 and m/z = 84 by the action of •OH and h+ as well as •O2 −, respectively. On the other hand, it decomposes to m/z = 79. In route Ⅲ, the hydroxylation reaction of MNZ results in the creation of m/z = 142. After this, 2 C (f− = 0.0311,f+ = 0.0461, f0 = 0.0386) and 5 H (f− = 0.0428,f+ = 0.0430, f0 = 0.0429) with high Fukui indices are attacked by •O2 − and •OH, and thus induce the production of m/z = 114 without a ring via a ring-opening reaction. Although 1 C, 3 C and 4 C also have high Fukui indices, their reactivity is affected by steric hindrance. After these intermediates are produced, they further form m/z = 56, 60 and 74 via ring-opening procedures driven by •O2 −, •OH and h+. Eventually, the MNZ will be degraded to carbon dioxide, water and nitrate. The combination of these inferences and the UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 4b verifies the reliability of the Fukui index and confirms that the intensive degradation of MNZ arises from the crucial functions of •O2 −, •OH and h+.

Toxicity assessment of the products was useful for weighing the validity and environmental significance of MNZ elimination. Considering this, the ecotoxicity of intermediates linked to MNZ degradation was appraised via Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.) software, and the parameters analyzed based on the quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) comprised bioconcentration factor, mutagenicity, acute toxicity LC50 and growth inhibition IGC50 (detailed data were summarized in Table S3) [58–60]. As presented in Fig. 11a, the bioconcentration factor follows a dramatic decreasing trend for all intermediates except m/z = 125, implying that the organisms are more likely to attain benign growth in the presence of these intermediates rather than with MNZ. Moreover, MNZ has a high mutagenicity value, and the mutagenicity values of the intermediates all decline after its decomposition (Fig. 11b). Despite the fact that primary intermediates (m/z = 185, 155, 142 and 125) present positive mutagenicity, the mutagenicity of the subsequent products all switches to negative as the elimination reaction continues further. The data illustrate that the environmental risk to organisms posed by the intermediates of MNZ is noticeably mitigated after treatment with Fe@C-Ti-2. In addition, the acute toxicity of m/z = 185 and 116 as primary products is slightly higher than that of MNZ (Fig. 11c). However, the remaining products are less toxic relative to MNZ and most of them exhibit harmless levels, revealing a remarkable detoxification effect of the system on MNZ. For growth inhibition (Fig. 11d), except for m/z = 185, whose inhibition level is somewhat above that of MNZ, the inhibition levels of all the other intermediates are lower than that of MNZ, where the inhibition levels of some degradation products (m/z = 74, 60 and 56) are even an order of magnitude below that of MNZ. These data support that the degradation intermediates of MNZ have a steep decline in their ability to suppress the growth of organisms versus MNZ. The above toxicity assessment results prove the potential environmental significance of the MNZ degradation with Fe@C-Ti-2, reflecting the obvious diminution of the bioconcentration factor, mutagenicity, acute toxicity and growth inhibition.

A catalyst composed of nanoscale zero valent iron encased with CQDs-doped b-TiO2 nanocrystals exhibited remarkable ability to degrade the persistent antibiotic MNZ via photo-elimination. The photo-elimination efficiency of MNZ on the composite (Fe0 @CQDs-TiO2(b) with 0.5 wt% CQDs) was 2.64, 8.25 and 1.34 times higher than that on pristine nZVI, b-TiO2 nanocrystal and Fe0 @b-TiO2, respectively. The enhanced degradation was credited to the interposition of CQDs and the in-situ fabrication of a Z-scheme heterojunction. The addition of CQDs induced the generation of •OH during the dark reaction, and narrowed and optimized the band gap of b-TiO2. And the light utilization ability of b-TiO2 and the isolation and translocation efficiency of its photo-generated charges during the photo-reaction were ameliorated. The passivation coating of nZVI was activated by visible-light into a semiconductor with superior visible-light absorption and a narrow band gap, and constructed the Z-scheme heterojunction with CQDs-TiO2(b). The composition of the oxide layer and the physicochemical properties of the composite were examined. And the composite held potential for practical employment via excellent resistance to interfering matrixes (anions, HA and actual water bodies). The elimination mechanism of MNZ was clarified thoroughly. Moreover, the intermediates and pathways for the deep degradation of MNZ were ascertained through the collaboration of theoretical calculations and LC-MS analysis, and the toxicity evaluation of the associated products was analyzed computationally, confirming that the final degradation products were all less toxic to the aquatic environment than MNZ. This work delivered creative insights into the unique photocatalytic potential of passivation coatings in nZVI-based catalysts and the multiple functions of CQDs for environmental restoration.

The misuse of metronidazole induces the emergence of antibiotic-resistance genes (ARGs), and consequently harms the food chain. For the deep elimination of this persistent pollutant, an in-situ assembled Z-scheme heterojunction catalyst (Fe0@CQDs-TiO2(b)) is easily synthesized. The insertion of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) stimulates the potential of Fenton-like reactions of nanoscale zero valent iron (nZVI, Fe0) and expands the optical properties of black TiO2 (b-TiO2). Moreover, the passivation layer of nZVI is activated by visible-light as a semiconductor and in-situ constructs a Z-scheme heterojunction with CQDs-TiO2(b). The material possesses potential for practical application because of its outstanding resistance to environmental matrices.

Xi Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing-original draft. Xiangyu Wang: Data curation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Resources. Iseult Lynch: Methodology, Supervision. Zhiling Guo: Visualization, Software. Peng Zhang: Visualization, Software. Lisi Wu: Investigation. Ping Ning: Writing – review & editing. Nanqi Ren: Writing – review & editing.
